coredata.dc:title: Surface plasmon resonance sensor based on gold-graphene quantum dots thin film as a sensing nanomatrix for phenol detection
coredata.dc:creator: Hashim, Hazwani Suhaila
Fen, Yap Wing

coredata.dc:description: Surface plasmon resonance sensor has sparked an interest among researchers as it offers a lot of advantages. However, this optical sensor encountered a drawback of poorly sensitive towards low concentration of target analytes. Hence, a modification was done on the gold thin film by incorporating a layer of graphene quantum dots. The sensing performance of the gold and gold-graphene quantum dots thin films was tested for phenol, which is a widely distributed pollutant in the environment. The surface plasmon resonance sensor based on the fabricated gold and gold-graphene quantum dots thin films were successfully carried out over a broad range of phenol from 0.1 to 1000 µM. Comparing the sensing performance of both thin films, lower limit of detection of 0.1 µM was achieved by the gold-graphene quantum dots thin film whereas gold thin film obtained a value of 1 µM. The gold-graphene quantum dots thin film also exhibited a higher sensitivity value of 0.02199° µM−1 for the phenol sensing. The findings provide evidence that the addition of graphene quantum dots to the gold thin film surface has significantly improved the phenol sensing capabilities of this optical sensor. In addition, the changes in the refractive index of the phenol solution as well as the refractive index and thickness of the gold-graphene quantum dots thin film were verified using the fitted SPR curves. The performance of the gold-graphene quantum dots thin film then was further analyzed using full width at half maximum, detection accuracy, and signal-to-noise ratio that demonstrated the potential of this surface plasmon resonance sensor for phenol detection. This gold-graphene quantum dots thin film-based SPR sensor also gave excellent selectivity towards phenol compared to other interfering substances. Additionally, atomic force microscopy proved the interaction between phenol and the thin film as a change in the RMS roughness can be observed.

head.ce:title: Surface plasmon resonance sensor based on gold-graphene quantum dots thin film as a sensing nanomatrix for phenol detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Surface plasmon resonance sensor has sparked an interest among researchers as it offers a lot of advantages. However, this optical sensor encountered a drawback of poorly sensitive towards low concentration of target analytes. Hence, a modification was done on the gold thin film by incorporating a layer of graphene quantum dots. The sensing performance of the gold and gold-graphene quantum dots thin films was tested for phenol, which is a widely distributed pollutant in the environment. The surface plasmon resonance sensor based on the fabricated gold and gold-graphene quantum dots thin films were successfully carried out over a broad range of phenol from 0.1 to 1000 µM. Comparing the sensing performance of both thin films, lower limit of detection of 0.1 µM was achieved by the gold-graphene quantum dots thin film whereas gold thin film obtained a value of 1 µM. The gold-graphene quantum dots thin film also exhibited a higher sensitivity value of 0.02199° µM−1 for the phenol sensing. The findings provide evidence that the addition of graphene quantum dots to the gold thin film surface has significantly improved the phenol sensing capabilities of this optical sensor. In addition, the changes in the refractive index of the phenol solution as well as the refractive index and thickness of the gold-graphene quantum dots thin film were verified using the fitted SPR curves. The performance of the gold-graphene quantum dots thin film then was further analyzed using full width at half maximum, detection accuracy, and signal-to-noise ratio that demonstrated the potential of this surface plasmon resonance sensor for phenol detection. This gold-graphene quantum dots thin film-based SPR sensor also gave excellent selectivity towards phenol compared to other interfering substances. Additionally, atomic force microscopy proved the interaction between phenol and the thin film as a change in the RMS roughness can be observed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Surface plasmon resonance (SPR) is an optical technique that have been extensively utilized for chemical and biochemical molecules detection [1–3]. This is due to the beneficial features offered by this sensor which include simple sample preparation, fast detection capability, label free analysis, and real time detection [4–9]. SPR sensor is a surface-sensitive technique that relies on its ability to monitor any refractive index changes that happen in the medium near to a metal film [10–14].

SPR works under total internal reflection condition, in which the angle of incidence must exceed the critical angle to vanish all the refraction light and leaving only reflected light [15,16]. When a light wave is directed through a prism towards a metal film, a portion of the light is sent back to the prism through reflection, while the remainder propagates within the metal film as an inhomogeneous electromagnetic wave. The electromagnetic wave will decay exponentially in the direction perpendicular to the interface between the prism and the metal film and can be known as the evanescent wave [17]. When the metal layer is thin enough, the evanescent wave can propagate along the metal-dielectric interface, leading to the excitation of free electrons on the metal surface [18–20]. Consequently, surface plasmon wave will be formed and propagates along the metal-dielectric interface [21,22]. Hence, resonance will occur at a specific angle of incidence due to the similarity in momentum of incident light and surface plasmon wave, observed as a sharp dip [23–27]. This specific incident angle is called as the resonance angle.

Copper, gold, aluminum, and silver are some of the options of noble metals in preparing the metal film for the fabrication of SPR sensor [28–31]. Among them, gold has been preferred by a lot of researchers as it is chemically stable. However, the capability of this gold film to detect low-concentration target analytes is not that powerful enough. In view of this challenge, there is an urgent need for a deposition of material onto the surface of gold thin film to enhance the sensing performance of the SPR sensor.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is a zero-dimensional nanomaterial that possess properties derived from both graphene and carbon dots [32–34]. GQDs possess excellent optical and electrical properties as a result of its prominent quantum confinement and edge effects [35–38]. Moreover, GQDs also display some exceptional characteristics such as large surface area, high stability, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and good solubility [39–44]. Thus, the application of GQDs in sensing application has ignited tremendous interests among researchers due to the above-mentioned beneficial features. GQDs have been extensively utilized in the fabrication of sensors to detect heavy metal ions [45,46], hydroquinone [47,48], hydrogen peroxide [49,50], glucose [51–53], nitrophenol [54–57], and phenol [58–60].

Phenol is an environmental pollutant that has been regarded as a hazardous substance by United States Environmental Protection Agency (US-EPA) [61,62]. Phenol has been widely released into the oceans through various manufacturing industries [63,64]. Phenol was famously known for its harmful threats towards human organs including lungs, liver, and kidneys [65–67]. Thus, several studies for sensing phenol using the SPR sensor have been reported that leads to the fabrication of sensing layers such as E. coli O157:H7 immobilized on cysteine-terminated synthetic oligopeptides-chromium-gold film [68], tyrosinase immobilized on polyacrylamide gel-silver film [69], hybrid polypyrrole-polystyrene sulfonate modified gold film [70], tyrosinase-graphene oxide immobilized on gold film [71], and phenol imprinted poly(2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate-N-methacryloyl-(L)-phenylalanine methyl ester) polymeric gold film [72]. However, the required costs for these reported works were relatively high as they incorporated a lot of materials for the development of the sensing materials. Besides, the preparation of the sensing materials consisted of several steps and some of it were complicated. In addition, the results obtained have not been proven theoretically.

Interestingly, GQDs have not yet been incorporated with the SPR sensor for sensing phenol, but have been studied using several other methods including resonance light scattering [58], photoluminescence [59], and differential pulse voltammetric [60]. Therefore, in this research, the deposition of GQDs on the gold thin film as the sensing nanomatrix incorporated with SPR sensor for the detection of phenol will be fabricated for the first time. This present work evaluates the potential detection of phenol using SPR sensor incorporated with gold-GQDs thin film. In order to validate the experimental results, the optical properties of the gold-GQDs thin film have been revealed using the WinSpall program. Furthermore, the sensing performance parameters of this fabricated sensor including sensitivity, full width at half maximum, detection accuracy, signal-to-noise ratio, and selectivity were evaluated. Besides, the optical properties of the proposed thin film have been studied using UV–Vis-NIR spectroscopy and the surface morphology of the thin film before and after in contact with phenol using atomic force microscopy was also presented.
GQDs (1 mg/mL) and phenol (MW = 94.11 g/mol) were obtained from ACS Material and Sigma-Aldrich, respectively. Glass substrates (24 mm × 24 mm × 0.1 mm) were bought from Menzel-Glaser, Germany.

By using dilution formula (M1V1 = M2V2 ), the 1 mg/mL GQDs was diluted to 0.5 mg/mL by adding deionized water. 0.1 M of phenol stock solution was prepared by diluting 0.188 g of phenol in 20 mL deionized water. Then, the stock solution was diluted into a lower concentration of 0.01 M also using the dilution formula. Afterwards, the 0.01 M phenol solution was further diluted to 1000 µM and the step was repeated until it reached the desired concentrations (100, 10, 1, and 0.1 µM) that will be used during the study.

Glass substrates were firstly cleaned to remove any fingerprints and dirt present on their surfaces. Next, by using SC7640 Sputter Coater, a layer of gold was deposited on the glass substrates. After that, about 1 mL of the GQDs solution was placed onto the surface of gold film, and then spun at 1000 rpm for 30 s using P-6708D spin coater.

UV–Vis-NIR spectroscopy (UV-3600 Shimadzu) was used to determine the absorbance of the gold-GQDs thin film in the range of 220–500 nm at room temperature. The surface morphology and RMS roughness of the gold-GQDs thin film before and after in contact with phenol were investigated using atomic force microscopy. Bruker AFM Dimension Edge ScanAsyst Peak Force in tapping mode with a scan size of 1 µm × 1 µm was used for this aim.

Fig. 1 shows the custom-built SPR optical sensor based on Kretschmann configuration that was used to study the sensing performance of gold or gold-GQDs thin films. Initially, the uncoated side of the thin film was thumbed against a prism (n = 1.77861) using index-matching fluid, meanwhile the gold or gold-GQDs film surface was attached to a measuring cell. Afterward, the prism and measuring cell were loaded onto a rotating stage. The SPR experiment began by introducing deionized water in the measuring cell so that it would come into contact with the sensor film to obtain the reference signal. The prism was exposed to a He-Ne laser beam, which then passed through the thin film of the sensor [73,74]. The reflected light will be captured by a photodiode and the signal will be analyzed using a lock-in amplifier [75,76]. Subsequently, the SPR experiment was resumed by replacing the deionized water in the measuring cell with varies concentrations of phenol solution starting from 0.1 to 1000 µM. The refractive index of the phenol solution and the properties of the thin film including the thickness and its refractive index were studied using WinSpall software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical study of gold-GQDs thin film
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 illustrates the UV–Vis absorption spectrum for the gold-GQDs thin film. Strong optical absorption in the UV region with a tail extending to the visible range can be observed. The highest peak that appeared at 274 nm can be attributed to the π–π* transition of the C = C bonds [77]. A shoulder peak at around 385 nm arises due to the n–π* transition of C = O group in GQDs [78]. By using this absorption spectrum, the value of optical bandgap energy of the gold-GQDs thin film can be determined with the aid of a plot of absorption energy (αhv, where α is the absorbance and hv is the photon energy) against hv as shown in the inset of Fig. 2 [79]. Thus, the optical bandgap energy of the gold-GQDs thin film was found to be 4.111 eV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SPR response for phenol on gold thin film
ce:section.ce:para: The gold thin film was firstly exposed to deionized water (DW) that gave a resonance angle value of 53.713°. Next, the measurement was proceed using different concentrations of phenol (0.1–1000 µM). The SPR reflectance for gold thin film when detecting the target analytes was plotted in Fig. 3 .

Interestingly, the resonance angle recorded for phenol solution at the concentration of 0.1 µM was the same as DW which is also 53.713°. This might be owing to the similarity in the refractive index of the 0.1 µM phenol solution with DW [80,81]. Besides, the phenol solution might contains only a little amount of phenol to be attached on the gold thin film [82,83]. As 1 µM phenol was introduced, the resonance angle has increased to 53.734°. The refractive index of the 1 µM phenol might be slightly higher than the 0.1 µM of phenol and DW causing the SPR curve to shift to the right. In this study, limit of detection is defined as the lowest concentration of target analyte that can be distinguished by the sensor from its baseline signal. Thus, this suggests that the limit of detection of this gold thin film for sensing phenol is 1 µM. As the concentrations of phenol were further increased to 10, 100 and 1000 µM, the resonance angles were also increased with values of 53.755°, 53.766°, and 53.776°, respectively. This might be due to the continuous increment in the refractive index of the phenol solution as its concentrations increased.

To validate the above observation, the SPR curves were fitted using the WinSpall software as shown in Fig. 4 . According to the fitted SPR curve of DW (refractive index = 1.3330), the real part, n and imaginary part, k of the gold layer was 0.165 and 3.740, respectively. The thickness of the gold layer was also obtained from the fitting which is 49.8 nm. Furthermore, the refractive index of the different concentrations of phenol were also analyzed using the fitted SPR curves and the data were tabulated in Table 1 . Based on the fitting result, the refractive index of the 0.1 µM phenol was exactly the same as that of the DW, which explains why there was no shift in the SPR curve between the DW and 0.1 µM phenol. Then, as the phenol concentrations increased from 1 to 1000 µM, the n values were slightly increased from 1.3333 to 1.3340. This has contributed to the increase in the resonance angle of the experimental SPR curve that can be observed as a shift to the right as in Fig. 3. The values of n, k, and thickness of the gold layer remained the same when it is in contact with all of the phenol concentrations.

In order to measure the sensitivity of the SPR sensor, resonance angle shift has been introduced as an important parameter [84]. The resonance angle shift for the gold thin film was measured by calculating the difference between the resonance angle of each of the phenol concentrations with the resonance angle of DW and has been tabulated in Table 2 . Next, a graph of resonance angle shift of gold thin film against the phenol solution in the range of 1–1000 µM with a linear fit was plotted as shown in Fig. 5 . The gradient of the linear fit is regarded as the sensitivity of this sensor as it is defined as the ratio between the resonance angle shift and measured target concentration. Hence, this gold thin film exhibited a sensitivity of 0.01153° M−1 for the detection of phenol with a correlation coefficient of 0.95465.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SPR response for phenol on gold-GQDs thin film
ce:section.ce:para: The SPR test then was continued using the gold-GQDs thin film. Same as before, the gold-GQDs thin film was let to be in contact with DW first. The obtained resonance angle for this gold-GQDs sensor film in contact with DW is found to be 53.723° which is a bit higher than the gold film. The presence of GQDs on top of the gold film is thought to have caused a change in the refractive index of the thin film [85]. Next, 0.1 µM of phenol solution was introduced to the gold-GQDs thin film and gave a small shift of resonance angle with a value of 53.728°. Hence, the limit of detection of the gold-GQDs thin film for sensing phenol using SPR sensor is 0.1 µM.

The SPR experiment then was further studied using following concentrations of phenol that were injected one after another. As can be seen in Fig. 6 , the SPR reflectance curves for the phenol have been shifted to the right with the increase in its concentrations. The resonance angle and resonance angle shift of the gold-GQDs thin film then have been tabulated in Table 3 . The resonance angle shift of this gold-GQDs thin film for each of the phenol concentrations was a little bit higher as compared to the gold film. It was mainly due to the attachment of the phenol onto the surface of the GQDs. The phenol and GQDs sensing layer can interact with each other through strong hydrogen bonding since the surface of GQDs contains a large number of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups [86–88]. In addition, the presence of aromatic rings in both phenol and GQDs can lead to π-π stacking interactions between them [60]. Hence, this binding between the GQDs and phenol will consequently change the refractive index of the gold-GQDs thin film leading to the higher resonance angle shift [89].

Fitting of the SPR curves was carried out to find the optical properties and thickness of the GQDs layer on top of the gold-GQDs thin film before and after in contact with the phenol as can be seen in Fig. 7 . The findings have been tabulated in Table 4 . From the table, it can be observed that the n value of the GQDs sensing layer has increased from 1.370 for DW to 1.374 after being in contact with phenol solution of 0.1 µM despite the similarity in the refractive index of both solutions. Interestingly, the n values continued to increase as the phenol concentrations increase, whereas the k values gave an opposite pattern. This can be attributed by the strong interaction between the evanescent fields and sensing medium, leading to an increase in the real part of the wave vector of surface plasmon wave. A higher refractive index leads to an increased magnitude of the real part of the wave vector of surface plasmon wave, hence the resonance condition will occur at a larger value of resonance angle [90]. These results proved that there are some interactions that happened between the GQDs and phenol. Similarly, the effect from the interactions also can be observed by the increase in the thickness of the GQDs layer from 8.7 nm to 9.8 nm as shown in Table 4 [91]. Besides, the decrement in the values of k of the GQDs sensing layer will results in the reduced energy loss caused by electronic damping.

The sensitivity of the gold-GQDs thin film for sensing phenol then was obtained by plotting a graph of resonance angle shift of the thin film against phenol as shown in Fig. 8 . The sensor response was increasing linearly to the phenol solution over a broad range of 0.1 to 1000 µM. The linear fit with a correlation coefficient of 0.98991 produces a slope of 0.02199° M−1 , which is the sensitivity value for the gold-GQDs thin film. The sensitivity of this gold-GQDs sensor film was higher compared to the gold film (0.01153° M−1). This gold-GQDs film also was able to detect the phenol at a lower concentration than the gold film with a value of 0.1 µM and 1 µM, respectively. Hence, this proved that the coating of the GQDs on the gold surface film was able to improve the SPR sensor performance towards phenol detection.

The obtained results from this work then were compared with other sensing methods that have been reported for the detection of phenol by using quantum dots as the sensing layer as shown in Table 5 . This GQDs-based SPR sensor gave a much lower limit of detection as compared to the one that was utilizing only GQDs as the sensing material. Furthermore, comparing the performance of this SPR sensor with other sensors that have done some modifications on the quantum dots with other materials, the exhibited detection limits were quite comparable to each other and some of it were even higher than this present study. This demonstrated that this GQDs-based SPR sensor has a superior sensing performance towards the detection of phenol despite the fact that it is being used alone. On top of that, it is also believed that this reported SPR sensor will has a promising future and its limit of detection can be further improved by incorporating a highly potential material with the GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance parameters of SPR for gold-GQDs thin film
ce:section.ce:para: Full width at half maximum (FWHM) is another important parameter to evaluate the accuracy and efficiency of the SPR sensor [94]. FWHM of the SPR reflectance curve was obtained by measuring its angular width at half of the maximum value. A smaller FWHM value corresponds to a sharper and narrower SPR curve, which is desirable for achieving higher detection accuracy [95]. The detection accuracy (DA) is inversely related to the value of FWHM [71]. Fig. 9 shows the changes of FWHM and DA against phenol concentrations.

As compared to the 0.1 µM phenol solution, a slight decrease in the FWHM value for the 1 µM phenol can be noticed from 1.928° to 1.924° that may be resulted from the increase in the binding event between the target phenol and sensing layer [96]. A significant increase in the FWHM value can be observed after the sensing of phenol from 1 µM to 100 µM with values of 1.924°, 2.003°, and 2.050° for 1 µM, 10 µM, and 100 µM, respectively. A possible explanation for this observation was related to the larger intensified internal loss that resulted from the increase in the thickness of GQDs layer, that leads to the damping of incident light in the sensing layer, which in turns causing the SPR curve to become wider [97,98]. The FWHM achieves its minimum value during the detection of 1000 µM phenol at 1.866°, resulted in the highest DA value of 0.536/°. This phenomenon can be attributed to the decrease in scattering centers for incident light caused by the reduction of grain boundaries in the thicker sensing layer [99]. The values of DA for the gold-GQDs thin film in contact with 0.1 µM, 1 µM, 10 µM, and 100 µM phenol were 0.519/°, 0.520/°, 0.499/°, and 0.488/°, respectively.

This proposed sensor can be used to study another essential parameter, namely the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), which considers both the resonance angle shift and DA values. SNR is measured using the equation below:
(1)
SNR =
Δ θ S P R
FWHM
= Δ θ S P R × D A
where ΔθSPR is resonance angle shift. Fig. 10 shows the SNR trend of the gold-GQDs thin film for detecting phenol. The SNR increases as the concentrations of phenol increase with values of 0.003, 0.014, 0.023, 0.036, and 0.049 for 0.1 µM, 1 µM, 10 µM, 100 µM and 1000 µM of phenol, respectively. This indicates that the noise of the SPR signal has been reduced owing to the increase in signal strength as the binding of phenol was maximized onto the sensor surface. Furthermore, it is noteworthy that the SNR plotting of this gold-GQDs thin film was very similar to the resonance angle shift plot in Fig. 8. This proved that the resonance angle shift has a more significant influence on the SNR value compared to the DA [100,101]. The values of FWHM, DA, and SNR for the detection of phenol using gold-GQDs thin film were recorded in Table 6 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity study of gold-GQDs thin film
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the proposed SPR sensor based on gold-GQDs thin film towards phenol relative to the possible interfering substances including ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), potassium ion (K+), copper ion (Cu2+), and zinc ion (Zn2+) has been investigated. The selectivity of this sensor was represented by the resonance angle shift of each of the target analytes with a concentration of 100 µM as shown in Fig. 11 . The results showed that the resonance angle shift for phenol was 3 times higher as compared to the other target analytes, proving the high selectivity of the gold-GQDs thin film-based SPR sensor towards the detection of phenol. It was probably due to stronger π-π stacking interactions between the phenol and GQDs as compared to AA and UA, facilitated by the stronger aromatic ring in phenol, which is benzene ring. Besides, the most known interaction between the GQDs and the heavy metal ions is the electrostatic interactions between the negative charge from GQDs and positive charge from metal ions [101]. However, these electrostatic interactions might be relatively weaker compared to the strong hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking interactions that occur between the phenol and GQDs, contribute to the higher selectivity of the gold-GQDs thin film for phenol compared to the K+, Cu2+, and Zn2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology of gold-GQDs thin film
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology features of the gold-GDQs thin film before and after in contact with phenol that have been studied using atomic force microscopy (AFM) are shown in Fig. 12 (a) and Fig. 12 (b), respectively. Fig. 12 (a) revealed the formation of spherical-shaped GQDs that has been uniformly dispersed on the surface of the gold thin film [102,103]. In addition, the RMS roughness of the gold-GDQs thin film was 1.13 nm. After coming into contact with the phenol solution, the surface morphology of the thin film did not show any big changes, but the RMS roughness value slightly decreased to 0.99 nm. This is most likely owing to the interactions that have happened between the surface of the thin film and phenol, thus altered the values of the RMS roughness.

In this study, a novel SPR sensor incorporated with gold-GQDs thin film was developed and successfully demonstrated for phenol sensing. The SPR reflectance curves for varies phenol concentrations (0.1–1000 µM) have been analyzed. The optical properties of the thin films when detecting various concentrations of phenol also have been theoretically demonstrated using WinSpall program. The gold-GQDs thin film showed a superior sensing performance compared to the gold thin film only, with a greater sensitivity of 0.02199° M−1 and a lower detection limit of 0.1 µM. In addition, the gold-GQDs thin film also presents a good selectivity towards phenol. This obtained data are very promising and the gold-GQDs thin film can acts as a base for future studies with the aim to enhance the sensitivity of the SPR sensor for phenol detection.
Not applicable.
